title: The Federal Authority to License Small Modular Reactors – Institute for Policy Integrity
text:
As interest in advanced nuclear technologies grows, understanding the legal regime underpinning the deployment of small modular reactors (SMRs) is critical. This report describes the legal and factual background behind the regulation of SMRs and explores a recent challenge to the authority of the Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) to license these reactors. At the heart of the litigation is whether SMRs qualify as "utilization facilities" under the Atomic Energy Act of 1954 — a question that determines whether the NRC has the authority to license them at all.
The report explains that the NRC acted reasonably in labeling all nuclear reactors as utilization facilities, that the 2024 ADVANCE Act indicates Congress understood the NRC to have authority to license SMRs as such, and that nearly all the reactors at issue in the litigation can reasonably be considered utilization facilities given the quantities of special nuclear material they use.
This report does not suggest that the current licensing regime for SMRs is beyond improvement. Rather, it explains the NRC's legal authority to license SMRs as utilization facilities, and contends that there are better ways than litigation to address any mismatch between the existing regulatory framework and current reactor technology. A collaborative solution among SMR users, developers, and the NRC is preferable to letting non-expert judges determine whether certain nuclear reactors are inherently too safe to be subject to federal licensing requirements. Any regulatory reform in this area must respect Congress's decision to empower the NRC to license facilities capable of affecting the common defense or public health.
